ccel.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

title: The Death of Arius.

source.title: NPNF2-02. Socrates and Sozomenus Ecclesiastical Histories

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Such was the supplication of Alexander.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Meanwhile the emperor, being desirous of personally examining Arius, sent for him to the palace, and asked him whether he would assent to the determinations of the Synod at Nicæa.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He without hesitation replied in the affirmative, and subscribed the declaration of the faith in the emperor’s presence, acting with duplicity.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The emperor, surprised at his ready compliance, obliged him to confirm his signature by an oath.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: This also he did with equal dissimulation.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The way he evaded, as I have heard, was this: he wrote his own opinion on paper, and carried it under his arm, so that he then swore truly that he really held the sentiments he had written.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: That this is so, however, I have written from hearsay, but that he added an oath to his subscription, I have myself ascertained, from an examination of the emperor’s own letters.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The emperor being thus convinced, ordered that he should be received into communion by Alexander, bishop of Constantinople.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: It was then Saturday, and Arius was expecting to assemble with the church on the day following: but divine retribution overtook his daring criminalities.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: For going out of the imperial palace, attended by a crowd of Eusebian partisans like guards, he paraded proudly through the midst of the city, attracting the notice of all the people.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: As he approached the place called Constantine’s Forum, where the column of porphyry is erected, a terror arising from the remorse of conscience seized Arius, and with the terror a violent relaxation of the bowels: he therefore enquired whether there was a convenient place near, and being directed to the back of Constantine’s Forum, he hastened thither.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: Soon after a faintness came over him, and together with the evacuations his bowels protruded, followed by a copious hemorrhage, and the descent of the smaller intestines: moreover portions of his spleen and liver were brought off in the effusion of blood, so that he almost immediately died.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: The scene of this catastrophe still is shown at Constantinople, as I have said, behind the shambles in the colonnade: and by persons going by pointing the finger at the place, there is a perpetual remembrance preserved of this extraordinary kind of death.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: So disastrous an occurrence filled with dread and alarm the party of Eusebius, bishop of Nicomedia; and the report of it quickly spread itself over the city and throughout the whole world.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: As the king grew more earnest in Christianity and confessed that the confession at Nicæa was attested by God, he rejoiced at the occurrences.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: He was also glad because of his three sons whom he had already proclaimed Cæsars; one of each of them having been created at every successive decennial anniversary of his reign.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: To the eldest, whom he called Constantine, after his own name, he assigned the government of the western parts of the empire, on the completion of his first decade.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: His second son Constantius, who bore his grandfather’s name, he constituted Cæsar in the eastern division, when the second decade had been completed.

verses.book: The Ecclesiastical History of Socrates Scholasticus.
verses.text: And Constans, the youngest, he invested with a similar dignity, in the thirtieth year of his own reign.
